meta.title: The history of the guitar

meta.originalTitle: The history of the guitar 吉他的历史

children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13, which are based on Reading Passage 1 below.
An overview of the origins of the modern guitar

The earliest stringed instruments currently known to archaeologists are bowl harps. For millennia, people made bowl harps using, for example, tortoise shells as resonators, with a bent stick for a neck and one or more gut or silk strings. The world's museums contain many such harps from the ancient Sumerian, Babylonian, and Egyptian civilisations. Around 2500-2000 BC, more advanced harps, such as the beautifully carved 11-stringed instrument found in the tomb of Queen Shub-Ad in ancient Mesopotamia, now modern-day Iraq, started to appear.

The tanbur* probably developed from the bowl harp. It was different from the bowl harp in that its neck was straightened out to allow the strings to be pressed down to create more notes. Tomb paintings and stone carvings in Egypt indicate that harps and tanburs – plus flutes and percussion instruments – were being played together 3,500-4,000 years ago. Archaeologists have also found many similar relics amongst the ruins of the ancient Mesopotamian civilisation. Many of these instruments have survived into modern times in almost unchanged form, for example, folk instruments of the region such as the Turkish saz and Afghan panchtar.

At 3,500 years old, the tanbur which belonged to the Egyptian singer Har-Mose is the earliest known example of this instrument. Har-Mose's tanbur had three strings and a plectrum suspended from the neck by a cord. The soundbox, which increased the volume, was made of beautifully polished cedarwood and covered in rawhide. It can be seen today at the Archaeological Museum in Cairo.

In order to distinguish guitars from other stringed instruments, it is helpful to have a broad definition of the guitar. Music expert Dr Michael Kasha defines a guitar as having ‘a long, fretted neck, flat wooden soundboard, ribs, and a flat back, most often with sides that curve inwards'. The oldest known visual representation of such an instrument is a stone carving at Alaca Huyuk in Turkey, which shows a 3,300-year-old instrument with a long neck and sides that clearly curve inwards.

The name 'guitar' comes from the ancient Sanskrit word for 'string' – ‘tar'. Many popular stringed instruments used in Central Asia today have existed in an unchanged form for several thousand years, as shown by archaeological finds in the area. Many have names that end in 'tar', with a prefix indicating the number of strings, such as the doter, a two-stringed instrument found in Turkestan, and the Persian three-stringed setar and four-stringed chartar. The Indian sitar almost certainly took its name from the setar, but over the centuries it evolved radically, following the Indians' own aesthetic and cultural ideals.

Tanburs and harps spread around the ancient world with travellers, merchants and seamen. The earliest guitar-like instruments to arrive in Europe had, most often, four strings. Many such instruments, and variations with from three to five strings, can be seen in mediaeval illustrated manuscripts. They were also carved in stone in European churches and cathedrals, from the first century AD through until the 13th century.

When the four-stringed Persian chartar arrived in Spain, however, it changed in form and construction, acquiring pairs of strings tuned to the same note instead of single strings. It became known as the chitarra. By the middle of the 14th century, the chitarra had become dominant, at least in most of Europe. The earliest known music for the eight-stringed chitarra was written in 16th-century Spain. The ten-string version first appeared in Italy at the same time, and gradually replaced the eight-stringed instrument. A further two strings first appeared in the 17th century, an innovation which guitar makers all over Europe quickly took up. However, this twelve-string arrangement gradually gave way to six single strings across the continent. The six-stringed guitar can thus be said to be a development of the twelve-string, rather than vice versa, as was thought previously.

At the beginning of the 19th century, the present-day guitar began to take shape, although bodies were still fairly small and narrow-waisted. The modern classical guitar first appeared in its current form in the mid-19th century, when the Spanish guitar maker Antonio Torres increased the size of the body, altered its proportions, and introduced the revolutionary fan-braced top**. His design radically increased the volume and improved the tone of the instrument, and very soon became the norm. This design has remained essentially unchanged to this day.

At the time when Torres made his breakthrough, German immigrants to America – among them Christian Friedrich Martin – began making guitars with X-braced tops. Steel strings, which became widely available several decades later in the early 1900s, offered the promise of a much louder guitar, but the increased tension was too much for the fan-braced top. The stronger X-braced top proved equal to the job, and quickly became the industry standard.

At the end of the 19th century, guitar manufacturer Orville Gibson added steel strings to a body constructed like a cello, a combination which produced more volume. The electric guitar was born when pickups were fitted to Hawaiian and jazz guitars in the late 1920s, but met with little success until 1936, when Gibson introduced its famous ES150 model.
: a long-necked stringed instrument with a small pear-shaped body
: a strengthening structure in the shape of a fan inserted into the soundbox
Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?
In boxes 1–6 on your answer sheet, write
if the statement agrees with the information
if the statement contradicts the information
if there is no information on this
The instrument found in Queen Shub-Ad's tomb is the world's oldest known version of a harp.
Today's Afghan panchtar is very similar to an ancient Mesopotamian instrument.
The Egyptian singer Har-Mose was an excellent tanbur player.
The Cairo Archaeological Museum contains many historic musical instruments.
The instrument carved in stone at Alaca Huyuk is consistent with Dr Michael Kasha's definition of a guitar.
The different instruments that appeared in medieval literature had the same number of strings.

explanationSection.text:
答案：FALSE
解析：A段先说最早的弦乐器是“bowl harps”，而舒布-阿德墓中的是“more advanced harps”（更先进的竖琴），时间更晚，因此并非“最古老”。
答案：TRUE
解析：B段说许多古代美索不达米亚乐器以“almost unchanged form”（几乎未变的形式）流传至今，并举例“the Afghan panchtar”，这与“very similar”意思一致。
答案：NOT GIVEN
解析：C段只说那把坦布尔属于哈尔-摩西，并未提及他的演奏水平（excellent）。
答案：NOT GIVEN
解析：C段只说哈尔-摩西的坦布尔（it）可以在该馆看到，并未说明该馆是否收藏有“many”（许多）其他乐器。
答案：TRUE
解析：D段在给出卡沙的定义后，紧接着说阿拉贾许育克的石刻是“The oldest known visual representation of such an instrument”，即符合该定义的乐器。
答案：FALSE
解析：F段明确提到中世纪手稿中可以看到有“from three to five strings”（三到五根弦）的变体，数量并不同。

items.text:
(1) 题目 1：The instrument found in Queen Shub-Ad's tomb is the world's oldest known version of a harp. （在舒布-阿德王后墓中发现的那件乐器是世界上已知最古老的竖琴。）
答案：FALSE
解析：A段先说最早的弦乐器是“bowl harps”，而舒布-阿德墓中的是“more advanced harps”（更先进的竖琴），时间更晚，因此并非“最古老”。

(2) 题目 2：Today's Afghan panchtar is very similar to an ancient Mesopotamian instrument. （现在的阿富汗潘奇塔尔与古代美索不达米亚的一种乐器非常相似。）
答案：TRUE
解析：B段说许多古代美索不达米亚乐器以“almost unchanged form”（几乎未变的形式）流传至今，并举例“the Afghan panchtar”，这与“very similar”意思一致。

(3) 题目 3：The Egyptian singer Har-Mose was an excellent tanbur player. （埃及歌手哈尔-摩西是一位出色的坦布尔演奏者。）
答案：NOT GIVEN
解析：C段只说那把坦布尔属于哈尔-摩西，并未提及他的演奏水平（excellent）。

(4) 题目 4：The Cairo Archaeological Museum contains many historic musical instruments. （开罗考古博物馆收藏有许多历史乐器。）
答案：NOT GIVEN
解析：C段只说哈尔-摩西的坦布尔（it）可以在该馆看到，并未说明该馆是否收藏有“many”（许多）其他乐器。

(5) 题目 5：The instrument carved in stone at Alaca Huyuk is consistent with Dr Michael Kasha's definition of a guitar. （阿拉贾许育克的石刻中的乐器与卡沙博士对吉他的定义一致。）
答案：TRUE
解析：D段在给出卡沙的定义后，紧接着说阿拉贾许育克的石刻是“The oldest known visual representation of such an instrument”，即符合该定义的乐器。

(6) 题目 6：The different instruments that appeared in medieval literature had the same number of strings. （中世纪文献中出现的不同乐器拥有相同数量的琴弦。）
答案：FALSE
解析：F段明确提到中世纪手稿中可以看到有“from three to five strings”（三到五根弦）的变体，数量并不同。

children.text: from the passage for each answer.
was a development of an earlier instrument called the
extra strings added in the 16th century in Italy
its shape a result of modifications, including a larger
changes produced better tone and greater
a well-known version was brought out by

explanationSection.text:
答案：chartar
解析：G段提到，当四弦的波斯“chartar”到达西班牙后，演变成了“chitarra”。
答案：body
解析：H段说托雷斯“increased the size of the body”。
答案：volume
解析：H段说他的设计“radically increased the volume and improved the tone”。
答案：America
解析：I段说“German immigrants to America ... began making guitars with X-braced tops”。
答案：steel
解析：I段提到“Steel strings... became widely available ... in the early 1900s”。
答案：pickups
解析：J段说电吉他的诞生是当“pickups were fitted to... guitars in the late 1920s”。
答案：Gibson
解析：J段说电吉他直到1936年“when Gibson introduced its famous ES150 model”才成功。

items.text:
(1) 题目 7：Chitarra - was a development of an earlier instrument called the 7 \___\___ （奇塔拉 - 是由一种更早的叫做7 \___\___的乐器发展而来）
答案：chartar
解析：G段提到，当四弦的波斯“chartar”到达西班牙后，演变成了“chitarra”。

(2) 题目 8：Classical guitar - its shape a result of modifications, including a larger 8 \___\___ introduced by Antonio Torres （古典吉他 - 其外形经过改造，包括由安东尼奥·托雷斯引入的更大的8 \___\___）
答案：body
解析：H段说托雷斯“increased the size of the body”。

(3) 题目 9：changes produced better tone and greater 9 \___\___ （这些改变带来了更好的音色和更大的9 \___\___）
答案：volume
解析：H段说他的设计“radically increased the volume and improved the tone”。

(4) 题目 10：X-braced top guitar - first made in 10 \___\___ in the mid-19th century （X形支撑面板吉他 - 19世纪中期首先在10 \___\___制造）
答案：America
解析：I段说“German immigrants to America ... began making guitars with X-braced tops”。

(5) 题目 11：strings made of 11 \___\___ became available around 1900 （约1900年，用11 \___\___制成的琴弦开始普及）
答案：steel
解析：I段提到“Steel strings... became widely available ... in the early 1900s”。

(6) 题目 12：Electric guitar - in the 1920s, 12 \___\___ were added to guitars （电吉他 - 在20世纪20年代，12 \___\___被加装到吉他上）
答案：pickups
解析：J段说电吉他的诞生是当“pickups were fitted to... guitars in the late 1920s”。

(7) 题目 13：a well-known version was brought out by 13 \___\___ （一个著名的版本由13 \___\___推出）
答案：Gibson
解析：J段说电吉他直到1936年“when Gibson introduced its famous ES150 model”才成功。
